other_observers: Joey Kellner, Glenn Walbek, and a number others I can't remember; first found and identified by Joey Kellner
description: the bird was a fairly small gull, slightly but noticeable smaller than nearby Ring-billed Gulls. The overall shape was similar to Ring-billed Gull, but with a more delicate look to it, possibly due to the small bill. The bill was shorter and more delicate than those on nearby Ring-billed Gulls, and was entirely yellow. The legs were yellow. The irides was dark.  The bird's head was extensively marked with dark streaks, concentrated as a large collar, and around the eye, giving the bird a "big-eyed" look. The mantle was dark medium gray, a couple of shades darker than nearby Ring-billed Gulls. The bird had a large and bold tertial crescent, and a small white crescent further up the mantle. The wingtips were black with noticeable white mirrors on the folded wing. I did not see the bird in flight, so cannot comment on the size of the P10 mirror. The underparts and tail were clean white.Behaviors: the bird was loafing on the edge of the ice fairly close to the shore with a mixed species gull flock.Call: none heardPlumage: adult basic
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
